title: How a Writer Uses AI Without Losing His Voice

paragraphs.zh: 而且没有人喜欢往这些地方发东西。总有那种 reply guys（动不动就来回复抬杠的人）冒出来。体验就是很差，threads 也很蠢，blue sky 上则像是所有人都在哭、都在抱怨。就，整个氛围都很怪，大家都像是被创伤过一样。所以我想要一个只属于会员的地方。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You have to pay to be there because you have to pay to be a member.
And I was like, you know, I had these these thoughts.
I always thought Twitter should be ephemeral.
I think there should be no archive for these things.
I'd be like, it's crazy.

paragraphs.zh: 你得付费才能进去，因为你得先付费成为会员。我当时想的是，你知道，我一直有这些想法。我总觉得 Twitter 应该是 ephemeral（短暂即逝的）。我认为这些东西就不该有 archive（存档）。我会觉得，这太疯狂了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: There's like a web one point o slash web two point o philosophy of everything needs to be archived and saved forever, and I kinda don't believe that.
In fact, one of my heresies, when I'm with Kevin Kelly doing our walks, like we have heresy night where you say something you sort of believe, but, like, people you respect don't believe maybe.
And my one of my heresies is I think the entire Internet should be deleted every two weeks.
And, like, you can archive it offline or whatever, but, like, the entire Internet itself every two weeks should just disappear.
And there could be like, did it forces a lot of, like, why would you do that or whatever?

paragraphs.zh: 有一种很像 web one point o / web two point o 的哲学，觉得一切都必须被存档、被永久保存，但我不太相信这个。事实上，我有一个“异端”观点：我和 Kevin Kelly 一起散步时，我们会有 heresy night，就是你说出一个自己某种程度上相信、但你尊重的人可能并不认同的看法。我的一个异端观点就是，我觉得整个 Internet 都应该每两周被删除一次。你可以把它离线存档之类的，但 Internet 本身每两周就应该彻底消失一次。这样一来就会逼出很多类似“你为什么要这么做”之类的思考。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: But I think there is kind of value to ephemerality in the digital world.
So anyway, our The Good Place Everything Disappears in a Week.
You can only post twice a day.
You can reply 20 times a day.
Photos are in one bit black and white until you click them, then they, like, fade into color.

paragraphs.zh: 但我觉得，在数字世界里，短暂性（ephemerality）某种程度上是有价值的。总之，我们的 The Good Place 里，一切内容一周后都会消失。你一天只能发两次帖。一天可以回复 20 次。照片起初都是 1-bit 的黑白效果，只有你点开它们之后，它们才会慢慢淡出并显出彩色。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And what else is there?
No algorithm.
It's just reverse cron.
It's just sweet.
I don't know.

paragraphs.zh: 还有什么来着？没有 algorithm（算法）。就是单纯按时间倒序排列。就是很美好。我也不知道。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: This is lovely.
It's like my favorite place on the Internet.
It's the only place I wanna post.
And people have now been joining my membership program just to get access to that, which I think is cool.

paragraphs.zh: 这地方太可爱了。它就像是我在 Internet 上最喜欢的地方。也是我唯一想发内容的地方。现在甚至已经有人专门为了获得它的访问权限而加入我的 membership program，我觉得这很酷。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So it seems like and part partly, you were doing this before, you know, the acceleration language model coding over the last year year or so.
So you're technical.

paragraphs.zh: 所以听起来，某种程度上，你在过去大约一年里 language model（语言模型）加速编程这波趋势之前，就已经在做这些了。所以你是懂技术的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I yeah.
I'm technical.
I mean, I built my membership software by hand, by myself.

paragraphs.zh: 对，是的。我懂技术。我的意思是，我的 membership software 是我亲手、自己一个人做出来的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Like, all

paragraphs.zh: 就是，完全——

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Like, I learned Flask.
I, like, learned how to set it up and set the roots up and everything.
The TGP, The Good Place, was the first thing I built when Claude Code was released.
So Claude Code came out.
Oh, that was another fable thing I did.

paragraphs.zh: 比如说，我学了 Flask。我学着怎么把它搭起来，怎么配置 routes，诸如此类。TGP，也就是 The Good Place，是我在 Claude Code 发布时做的第一个东西。所以 Claude Code 一出来——哦，那也是我做的另一件 fable 风格的小事。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I had it refactored TGP.
So I was like, oh, look.
You made this a year ago, version one of you.
Can you refactor this and actually pull out the module?
It was one giant Python file, like 10,000 lines or whatever, and so it's now modulized because because I I wanna wanna expand it.

paragraphs.zh: 我让它重构了 TGP。所以我当时想，哦，你看，这是“一年前版本的你”做出来的 version one。你能不能把它重构一下，真正把 module 拆出来？它原来就是一个巨大的 Python 文件，大概有 10,000 行之类的，所以现在已经模块化了，因为——因为我想把它继续扩展。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So I wanna I wanna build a very good reads.
So like Goodreads

paragraphs.zh: 所以我想，我想做一个 very good reads。就像 Goodreads 那样。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: that Not great reads.

paragraphs.zh: 但不是 great reads。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Yeah.
Not great reads.
Very good reads.
That like so basically, Goodreads that also doesn't suck.
Again, like all these platforms just become so toxic.

paragraphs.zh: 对，不是 great reads，是 very good reads。就是，本质上说，是一个 Goodreads，但同时又不那么烂。还是那句话，这些平台最后都会变得特别有毒。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So yeah.
Well, this is a good example of like n of one software.
You know?
And and, you know, I think the obviously, the business models of of social media are such that it just becomes the the shitty place it is today.
You know?

paragraphs.zh: 所以，是的。这其实就是一个很好的 n of one software（单人定制软件）例子。你懂吧？而且，你知道，我觉得很明显，social media（社交媒体）的 business model（商业模式）决定了它最终会变成今天这种烂地方。你知道吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: It's like it it's forced by the business models at this, like, growth at all costs and, you you know, and, like, engagement is there's no qualitative assessment of engagement.
It's just engagement is good.
Yeah.
You know?
But it turns out most engagement is the the way to get most engagement is to be toxic.

paragraphs.zh: 就像，这是被它的 business model（商业模式）逼出来的——一切都以增长为代价，你知道的，而且像 engagement（互动）这种东西，根本没有对互动质量的评估，只是默认 engagement 就是好事。对吧？但结果是，想获得最多 engagement 的方式，往往就是变得有毒。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Right?
So I think what we're gonna see is this great pressure on these companies.
So, like, Campaign Monitor, Mailchimp.
Like, look, I'm also not their their target consumer.
It's businesses.

paragraphs.zh: 对吧？所以我觉得，我们将会看到这些公司面临很大的压力。比如说 Campaign Monitor、Mailchimp。你看，我也不是他们的目标用户，他们的客户是企业。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: They kinda don't care about people like us.
So being able to do this out of one software, and I think it's only gonna get easier.

And also, like, corollary is, like, you're gonna have it's so easy it's much easier to start a company that is like, hey, here's Campaign Monitor for like half the price, and we handle all the bullshit.
It's like, of course.
Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 他们其实不太在乎我们这种人。所以能够用 n of one software（单人定制软件）来做这件事，而且我觉得这只会越来越容易。还有一个相关推论是，开一家这样的公司也会容易得多：比如说，“嘿，这里有个 Campaign Monitor 的替代品，价格只要一半，而且那些乱七八糟的事都由我们处理。”这不是很自然吗？对啊。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Why not?
Like, because you can just put it together.
And you've got Supabase and all the you know, it's like all these there's all these pieces now where you don't need infrastructure investment, and it can scale with revenue effortlessly.
And then you just have an agent in the background doing that scaling and setting it all.
So I I think we're gonna enter this sort of golden age of tool building.

paragraphs.zh: 为什么不呢？因为你完全可以把它拼起来。你已经有 Supabase 和各种现成的东西了，现在这些模块都已经在那儿了，你不需要做基础设施投资，而且它还能随着营收轻松扩展。然后你再让一个 agent（智能代理）在后台负责扩容和全部配置。所以我觉得，我们将要进入这样一个工具构建的黄金时代。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: There's an n of one version of it, and then also there's just gonna be more competition in the marketplace forcing more innovation.
I think this is gonna be I think it net net pretty good except for the incumbents.

paragraphs.zh: 一方面会有一种 n of one 的版本，另一方面，市场上也只会出现更多竞争，从而迫使更多创新。我觉得这总体上、综合来看会相当不错，除了对 incumbents（既有巨头）之外。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: What does it mean for for, like, other writers?
And I just wanna move your mic a little bit because it's, like, kinda in your Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 这对比如说其他写作者意味着什么？还有，我想稍微挪一下你的麦克风，因为它有点在你的……对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I can't really see.

paragraphs.zh: 我其实看不太见。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Maybe There we go.

paragraphs.zh: 也许……好了，这样可以了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Move this way a

paragraphs.zh: 往这边挪一

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: little bit.
Yeah.
There you go.
Okay.
So but what does that mean for writers like yourself?

paragraphs.zh: 点。对，就是这样。好，所以这对像你这样的写作者意味着什么呢？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Like, people who are making a living online?
Is that do you think n of one software is the sort of the next frontier?
Like, subscription businesses were the, like, big thing over the last, like, five years.
Everyone's like, wanna start Substack.
Do you pay newsletters?

paragraphs.zh: 比如说，那些在网上谋生的人？你觉得 n of one software 算不算是下一个前沿？就像 subscription businesses（订阅制业务）在过去大概五年里一直是那种很大的趋势。每个人都在说，想开一个 Substack。你会为 newsletters（新闻简报）付费吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yes.
Is this, like, the logical next step for writers?

paragraphs.zh: 会。所以，对写作者来说，这是不是合乎逻辑的下一步？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Well, I mean, I think Substack, their innovation was not even the subscription thing.
Their innovation was you can email everyone for free.
Yeah.
That's like people don't talk about that enough.
Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 嗯，我的意思是，我觉得 Substack 的创新甚至都不是订阅这件事。他们的创新是你可以免费给所有人发邮件。对。这一点大家讨论得还远远不够。对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: That's the true sort of, like, piece de resistance of, like, what Substack was doing.
It's like, oh, you can import 10,000 addresses and send unlimited newsletters for free.
That's sort of a that's the value prop of that is quite good because that that's the real, like, competition against, like, Mailchimp or whatever.
And then the seamless subscription stuff was, like, also nice.
But I think most sub stacks don't make any money.

paragraphs.zh: 这才真正有点像 Substack 所做事情里的 piece de resistance（点睛之笔/压轴亮点）。就是，哦，你可以导入 10,000 个地址，然后无限量免费发送 newsletter（电子邮件简报）。这某种意义上才是它的 value prop（价值主张），而且这个价值主张相当强，因为这才是真正对标 Mailchimp 之类产品的竞争点。至于那种无缝的订阅功能，当然也不错。但我觉得大多数 sub stacks 都赚不到钱。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So really, it's that that is that was the value prop.
But I don't know how much n of one software is gonna be produced because I think most people don't need this level of specificity.
Yeah.
And as a writer too, like, I'm in this weird position where I have a really strong technical background, but my interest isn't building companies.
And I love literature.

paragraphs.zh: 所以说，真正的 value prop 就是那个。但我不知道会有多少 n of one software 被做出来，因为我觉得大多数人并不需要这么高程度的特异性。对。而且作为一个写作者，我也处在一种很奇怪的位置：我有非常强的技术背景，但我的兴趣并不是创业建公司。我热爱文学。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I love writing.
I love books.
I think it is one of the greatest things you can commit your life to.
That's why I've been doing that.
Just the forcing function of collimating thought, the forcing function of going to print.

paragraphs.zh: 我热爱写作。我热爱书。我觉得这是你可以用一生去投入的最伟大的事情之一。所以我一直在做这个。那种把思考校准、聚焦起来的 forcing function（强制机制），那种把内容真正送去印刷的 forcing function。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I think actually going to print is really critical part of bookmaking.
So, you know, all these factors, don't go away because of AI.
I would like to believe that I mean, the things that are changing is the publishing industry is way weaker than it used to be.
It doesn't bring as much value to the table.
And so writers are expected to kind of bring an audience in a way that they never were before.

paragraphs.zh: 我其实觉得，真正去印刷是 bookmaking（书籍制作）里非常关键的一部分。所以，你知道，所有这些因素都不会因为 AI 而消失。我愿意相信的是，正在变化的事情是：publishing industry（出版行业）比以前弱得多了，它能带来的价值也没以前那么大了。因此，写作者现在被期待自己带来受众，而这种情况是他们以前从未被这样要求过的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And so hopefully, what AI coding, what this kind of Cambrian explosion of software will bring is more tools for writers to be able to build better audiences.
And that will hopefully lead to, you know, more people being able to make a living as a writer.

paragraphs.zh: 所以我希望，AI coding，以及这种软件领域的 Cambrian explosion（寒武纪大爆发），会给写作者带来更多工具，让他们能够建立起更好的受众群体。而这也希望能让更多人可以靠写作为生。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Are you using it in your writing?

paragraphs.zh: 你会把它用在你的写作里吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: No. No. I use it as a research assistant.
So, like, I'm like, oh, okay.
Here's a building.

paragraphs.zh: 不会。不会。我把它当作 research assistant（研究助手）来用。比如说，我会想，哦，好，这里有一栋建筑。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I wanna write about this building.
Go out and find every blog post that has talked about this building.
Summarize it.
Give me all the links so I can go and check all the sources and read read the read the posts that seem most interesting.
I have to do things like that.

paragraphs.zh: 我想写这栋建筑。去找出所有谈过这栋建筑的 blog post，把它们总结一下。把所有链接都给我，这样我就能去核查所有来源，读一读那些看起来最有意思的帖子。我得做这类事情。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And, like, I'll I'll put it in, like, TKs and stuff like that.
Like, oh, yeah.
You know, who is the drummer on this album?
You know, TK.
And, like, I'll sort of dump those those menu those those pages into it and just giving all the TKs, and then I kinda go and, like, add them or I, like, modify as needed or whatever.

paragraphs.zh: 然后，像是，我会把一些 TKs 之类的东西放进去。比如，哦，对，你知道这张专辑的 drummer 是谁吗？你知道，TK。然后，我会把那些菜单页、那些页面之类的内容一股脑丢进去，先把所有 TKs 给出来，接着我再去把它们补上，或者按需要修改之类的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: But I don't I still don't I don't ever want it touching the writing because that's that that for me, that's the point of is being in there.
It's being in the mess of the writing.
And I think even, like, adding links and TKs and stuff like that is is I wanna be thinking about how they they're added to it.
But what what I do use it for is, like, cultural sensitivity checks.

So, like I was writing about Nagasaki the other day and I was like, oh, like, wanna make sure I'm writing about Nagasaki and referencing the atomic bomb, like where it hit, how it hit, you know, who was affected in a sensitive way, like appropriately.

paragraphs.zh: 但我不，我还是不，我绝对不想让它碰写作本身，因为对我来说，那才是重点，就是要待在里面，待在写作的混乱之中。而且我觉得，哪怕只是加链接、加 TKs 之类的东西，我也希望自己去思考它们该怎么加进去。不过我确实会拿它来做 cultural sensitivity checks（文化敏感性检查）。比如我前几天在写 Nagasaki，我就会想，哦，我得确保自己在写 Nagasaki、提到 atomic bomb 的时候——它落在哪里、怎么落下、你知道，谁受到了影响——是以一种敏感、得体的方式来写。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And so that is pretty amazing, where I used to ping friends about stuff like that.

paragraphs.zh: 所以这点相当惊人，因为以前遇到这种事我会去 ping 朋友。

sentences.text: What is your experience like, okay.
So you're making all this n o one software.
Mhmm.
You're using it as a research assistant.
Mhmm.

paragraphs.zh: 那你的体验是什么样？好，所以你在做这些 n o one software。嗯哼。你把它当 research assistant（研究助手）来用。嗯哼。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: What is your experience now day to day in your in your work life?
Are you like, oh, shit.
Now I'm a sysadmin for like four different pieces of software that I have to like send to agents to go fix?
Or is it is your work life still fairly similar to what it used to be?

paragraphs.zh: 那你现在日常工作里的体验是什么样？你会不会是那种，哦，妈的，我现在成了四五个不同软件的 sysadmin（系统管理员），还得把问题发给 agents（代理）让它们去修？还是说，你的工作状态跟以前相比其实还挺相似的？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: No. I'm not I mean, my my membership software just works.
Yeah.
You know, it's so simple, like, when you break it down.
I mean, this is why I'm using Python.

paragraphs.zh: 不会。我不是——我是说，我的 membership software 就是正常运作。对。你知道，把它拆开来看，其实特别简单。我的意思是，这也是为什么我用 Python。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I'm not using there's no, like, a what's it called?
There's there's no I can't go with

paragraphs.zh: 我没有在用——没有那种，呃，叫什么来着？没有，我没法接着说下去。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: React next.

paragraphs.zh: 接下来是 React。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: There's no React.
There's no next.
There's none of that stuff.
It's just it it's very simple.
And the point is is because I don't wanna be system doing anything.

paragraphs.zh: 其实没有 React。也没有 next。那些东西都没有。就是——它非常简单。关键在于，我不想让系统去做任何事。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And even with the newsletter software, that piece is only touched when I send newsletters.
You know what I mean?
Yeah.
So the sign ups are happening and that, like, that has to be working and it seems to be working.
I can check every couple days.

paragraphs.zh: 即使是 newsletter 软件，那一部分也只有在我发送 newsletter 时才会被碰到。你明白我的意思吗？对。所以注册一直在发生，而那一块——它必须能正常工作，而且看起来确实在正常工作。我每隔几天就可以检查一次。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: But it's the only critical thing is, like, when I hit send, make make sure that goes out.
And that only happens once a week or twice a week or something like that.
So I I don't feel like I'm I'm maintaining anything.
And actually, I get a high off of building things that allow me to write.
Like, so software that helps me write or that helps me publish in a new way inspires me to do more writing.

paragraphs.zh: 但唯一真正关键的事情是，比如，当我点击 send 的时候，要确保它能发出去。而那只会每周发生一次、两次，或者类似这样的频率。所以我并不觉得自己在维护什么东西。实际上，构建那些能让我写作的东西，会让我很兴奋。也就是说，那种帮助我写作、或者帮助我以新方式发布内容的软件，会激励我写更多东西。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So it feeds back into it in a positive way.

paragraphs.zh: 所以它会以一种正向的方式形成反馈。

sentences.text: How is that?

sentences.text: Yeah.
